claim: Catch the narrowest exception type that you can handle meaningfully; a bare except or broad Exception catch hides bugs and swallows KeyboardInterrupt-adjacent signals.
Validate inputs at system boundaries (API handlers, file readers, CLI parsing) and raise immediately with a message naming the bad value; never let invalid data travel deeper before failing.
When re-raising a transformed exception, use 'raise NewError(...) from original' so the causal chain survives into the traceback.
In finally blocks and __exit__ methods, never let cleanup errors mask the original exception; log cleanup failures and let the primary error propagate.
Acquire files, locks, connections, and sessions with context managers (with statements) so release is guaranteed on every exit path, including exceptions.
Never use mutable values (lists, dicts, sets) as default parameter values; default to None and create the object inside the function.
Type-annotate every public function signature and dataclass field; annotations are executable documentation and let tooling catch interface drift.
Use comprehensions for simple map/filter transforms and explicit loops once logic needs branching or side effects; a comprehension with nested conditions is worse than a loop.
In matplotlib, always create figures and axes explicitly (fig, ax = plt.subplots()) and operate on the axes object; return the axes when a function produces a plot.
Build filesystem paths with pathlib.Path and the / operator, never string concatenation; it handles separators, and methods like .resolve() and .relative_to() prevent traversal bugs.
In async code, route CPU-bound or blocking-IO work through an executor (run_in_executor, to_thread); a single blocking call in a coroutine stalls every other task on the loop.
Declare direct dependencies with floor versions in pyproject.toml and put optional capability groups behind extras so consumers install only what they need.
Every promise must be awaited, returned, or explicitly fire-and-forgotten with error handling attached; a floating promise turns failures into silent nothing.
Use === and !== everywhere; loose equality's coercion table is a bug generator, and the rare intentional coercion deserves an explicit conversion instead.

key: General|JavaScript|State|Treat props and state as immutable
claim: In React and similar frameworks, never mutate props or state in place; produce new objects/arrays so change detection and time-travel debugging keep working.
Type external data (API responses, JSON parses, form input) as unknown and narrow it with validation before use; trusting a cast on external data moves the crash deeper into the app.

key: General|JavaScript|Modules|No side effects at import time
claim: Module top-level code should only define things; side effects at import time (network calls, registrations that log, environment reads) make load order a hidden dependency.
Name the columns you need instead of SELECT *; star queries break when schemas evolve, defeat covering indexes, and move more bytes than the caller uses.
Pass values as bound parameters, never by string-formatting them into SQL; interpolation is the injection vector and also defeats statement caching.

key: General|SQL|Indexes|Index what you filter and join on
claim: Create indexes on columns used in WHERE, JOIN, and ORDER BY for hot queries, and verify with the query plan; an unused index costs writes while an absent one costs reads.
Open transactions as late as possible and commit as early as correctness allows; long-lived transactions hold locks, block vacuum/compaction, and turn contention into timeouts.
Never edit an applied migration; write a new one. Applied migrations are shared history, and rewriting them desynchronizes every database that already ran them.
NULL compares as unknown, not false: use IS NULL / IS NOT NULL, remember NOT IN with a NULL list matches nothing, and aggregate functions silently skip NULLs.
Every error path either fully recovers (and the caller can't tell) or propagates loudly with context; the forbidden middle is catching, logging at debug, and continuing in a corrupt state.
An error message states what was expected, what was found, and the most likely fix or next step; 'invalid input' costs the reader a debugging session that 'expected HH:MM, got 25:00' prevents.
Retry with backoff and jitter, only for transient failures, only on idempotent operations; retrying a non-idempotent write turns one failure into duplicate effects.

key: General|Errors|Timeouts|Every external call gets a timeout
claim: Network calls, subprocess runs, and lock acquisitions all get explicit timeouts; an operation without a timeout is a hang waiting for its moment.
Assert on observable behavior (outputs, state changes, emitted calls at the boundary) rather than internal structure, so refactors that preserve behavior don't break tests.
Tests must not depend on execution order or share mutable state; each test builds its own fixtures and could run alone, first, or last with the same result.
Empty inputs, single elements, maximum sizes, duplicates, unicode, and None are where code breaks; write those cases before the happy path, which usually works by construction.

key: General|Testing|Determinism|No real time no real randomness
claim: Inject clocks and random sources so tests control them; a test that reads the real clock or real entropy will eventually flake, and flaky tests train people to ignore failures.

key: General|Testing|Verification|Green tests are the only done
claim: A change is finished when the verification suite passes, not when the code looks right; run the tests after every change and treat the exit code as the record.

key: General|API|Contracts|Be conservative in output liberal in input
claim: Accept slightly messy input where unambiguous (trailing slashes, case in enums) but emit strictly canonical output; consumers build on what you emit.
Additive changes (new optional fields, new endpoints) are safe; renames, removals, and semantic changes require a new version and a deprecation window.
API errors carry a stable machine-readable code, a human message, and the offending field where applicable; clients branch on the code, never on message text.
Any endpoint returning a collection that grows with usage gets pagination from day one; retrofitting pagination onto consumers that assume completeness is a breaking change.

key: General|Security|Secrets|Secrets never enter code or logs
claim: Credentials live in environment variables or secret managers, never in source, config files in the repo, error messages, or logs; scrub environment before spawning child processes.
Validate, bound, and escape everything that crosses a trust boundary: user input, file contents, API responses, environment variables, filenames; the question is never whether to validate but where.

key: General|Security|Least privilege|Grant the minimum that works
claim: Processes, tokens, and database users get the narrowest permissions that let them function; a read path with write credentials is a breach amplifier.
Resolve paths (symlinks, .., relative segments) BEFORE checking them against allow/deny rules; checking the raw string lets ../ and links walk around the rule.
Prefer message passing, queues, and ownership transfer over shared mutable state with locks; when locks are unavoidable, define a strict acquisition order to prevent deadlocks.
Any check-then-act sequence on shared state (exists-then-create, read-then-update) races; use atomic operations, upserts, or compare-and-swap that combine the check and the act.
Unbounded queues convert overload into memory exhaustion later instead of backpressure now; pick a bound and a policy (block, drop, reject) deliberately.
Profile before optimizing: the slow part is rarely where intuition points, and an optimization without a measurement is a bet placed blind.

key: General|Performance|Complexity|Mind the loop inside the loop
claim: Most real-world slowness is an accidental O(n*m): a lookup, query, or API call inside a loop; hoist it, batch it, or index it before reaching for micro-optimizations.
N+1 query patterns dominate service latency; fetch related rows with joins or IN-lists, and batch writes into multi-row statements or transactions.
Never add a cache without deciding how it invalidates (TTL, event, content-hash); a cache without an invalidation story is a stale-data bug with good latency.
Each commit contains one logical change with a message stating what and why; mixed commits make bisects, reverts, and reviews all worse at once.
Do work on short-lived branches and integrate frequently; long-lived divergence converts merge day into archaeology.
Rebase and amend freely on private branches, never on anything pushed and shared; rewriting shared history strands every collaborator's local state.
Modules interact through narrow, explicit interfaces (function signatures, schemas, queues); reaching into another module's internals couples you to what it never promised.

key: General|Design|Data shape|Question data shape before model choice
claim: Before choosing an algorithm, library, or storage model, characterize the data: size, cardinality, growth, access pattern, mutability; the right structure for the actual shape beats a clever algorithm on the wrong one.

key: General|Design|Failure modes|Design around failure modes first
claim: Enumerate how a component fails (timeout, partial write, duplicate delivery, crash mid-operation) and design the recovery before the happy path; retrofitted error handling never covers the real cases.
Choose the simplest tool that meets the actual requirement; every novel component must pay for its operational cost, and most don't.
Design writes and handlers to be idempotent (natural keys, upserts, dedup tokens) so retries, replays, and at-least-once delivery are safe instead of scary.
Validate configuration completely at startup and refuse to start with a message naming the bad key; a config error discovered at request time is an outage, at startup it's a deploy fix.
Keep changes reviewably small; past a few hundred lines, review quality collapses to skimming, and 'looks good' stops meaning anything.
Choose names that state role and intent (retry_budget, stale_after); the reader's comprehension speed, not the writer's typing speed, is what naming optimizes.
Code states what; comments earn their place by stating why — the constraint, the rejected alternative, the bug this guards against; delete comments that paraphrase the line below.

key: General|Review|Dead code|Delete dead code do not fence it
claim: Remove unused code, parameters, and flags entirely; version control remembers it, while commented-out and unreachable code actively lies to readers.
Log discrete events with structured fields (ids, counts, durations) at appropriate levels; prose logs that interpolate everything resist the queries you'll need during an incident.
Emit latency, traffic, errors, and saturation per component from day one; an incident without metrics is debugging by anecdote.
Every deploy needs a tested rollback path (previous artifact, reversible migration, feature flag); the time to discover a rollback doesn't work is never during the incident.
Change schemas and interfaces in three deploys: add the new alongside the old, migrate consumers, then remove the old; simultaneous change-and-remove breaks whoever deploys second.
